g.data-narration: Welcome to our exploration of Interrupt Latency in Microcontrollers, a critical concept for embedded systems design.
Interrupt latency is the time between an interrupt request and the first ISR instruction execution. This delay determines system responsiveness.
In safety-critical systems like automotive controls, excessive latency causes system failures, data loss, and potentially catastrophic consequences.
Interrupt latency has five main components: detection, completion, context saving, vector fetching, and ISR entry latency.
Detection latency depends on interrupt mechanism. Completion latency requires waiting for the current instruction, which varies by instruction complexity.
g.text: Time for processor to detect interrupt occurred.
Must finish current instruction before responding.

g.data-narration: Context saving stores processor state to stack. Vector fetching loads the ISR address. These add 2-4 machine cycles each in 8051.
g.text: Save registers, program counter, status flags
Fetch interrupt vector address, load PC
Stack: PC → PSW → ACC → B → Registers...

g.data-narration: Architecture type, instruction complexity, priority conflicts, interrupt states, and stack operations all significantly impact latency.
g.text: Flash Wait States: External memory access can significantly increase latency at high frequencies

g.data-narration: The 8051 has 5 interrupt sources with fixed vector addresses. One machine cycle equals 12 oscillator periods.
The total latency formula combines instruction completion cycles, vector fetch cycles, and context saving cycles multiplied by machine cycle time.

text.tspan: Total Latency = (Completion + Vector + Context) × Machine Cycle Time

g.data-narration: Let's calculate latency for 8051 at 11.0592MHz. Minimum: 5 cycles ≈5.4μs. Maximum with MUL: 10 cycles ≈10.9μs.
With nested interrupts, higher priority interrupts preempt lower priority ISR, reducing effective latency for critical events.
g.text: Complete ISR instr + Save ISR + Fetch Timer0 + Save Timer0

g.data-narration: Techniques to reduce latency include interrupt nesting, register banking, fast context saving, DMA usage, and proper priority assignment.
Remember: 1 machine cycle equals 12 oscillator periods. Total latency = completion + vector + context save cycles times machine cycle time.
For exams: Memorize the 8051 machine cycle formula, distinguish minimum vs maximum latency, and remember the five latency components.
g.text: Practice calculations with different clock frequencies and instruction types!
